facts.label: WHY THEY WEAR ORANGE
facts.story: The Netherlands first wore orange at the 1934 World Cup — orange shirt, white shorts, light blue socks — and never switched back. The color owes nothing to the flag, which is red-white-blue; it traces to William of Orange, the 16th-century prince who led the Dutch revolt against Spain and fathered the nation. Ninety years later, the shirt is still called 'Oranje,' a jersey named for a prince who never touched a football, tying every World Cup squad to a royal house four centuries old.

facts.label: CRUYFF'S TWO STRIPES
facts.story: Every teammate wore Adidas' three stripes in 1974, but Johan Cruyff had a personal Puma deal and refused to advertise a rival's logo. The KNVB and Adidas relented, stitching him a one-off shirt with just two stripes down the sleeves, shorts, and socks. Cruyff wore it all the way to the final in Munich, turning a sponsorship dispute into the most recognizable jersey of the tournament — proof that even Total Football's biggest star answered to no one but himself.

1934.headline: One Match, Then Gone
1934.story: Netherlands' first-ever World Cup match was also their last: Kick Smit and Leen Vente both scored in Milan, but Switzerland answered twice more and won 3–2. There was no group stage to fall back on — a single elimination game, a single afternoon, and Oranje's tournament was over before it had really started.
1934.homeColors: orange shirt, white shorts, light blue socks

1938.headline: Blanked By Czechoslovakia
1938.story: Another single-elimination format, another early exit. Netherlands couldn't find a way past Czechoslovakia in Le Havre, going scoreless through 90 minutes and extra time before conceding three times late in the game. It remains the only World Cup match in Dutch history in which they failed to score at all.
1938.homeColors: orange shirt, white shorts, light blue socks

1974.headline: Total Football, Silver Medal
1974.story: Johan Cruyff's Total Football swept through the tournament: Argentina thrashed 4–0, Brazil beaten 2–0 in a second-round decider. Then, in the Munich final, the Dutch scored before West Germany even touched the ball — Johan Neeskens converting a penalty inside two minutes — only to concede twice and lose 2–1, the most painful near-miss in Dutch football history.
1974.homeColors: orange shirt with black trim, black shorts, orange socks (Adidas)

1978.headline: Nanninga's Header, Argentina's Party
1978.story: Rob Rensenbrink's five group-stage goals carried the Dutch, without an injured Cruyff, to a second straight final, this time in Buenos Aires. Substitute Dick Nanninga headed a late equalizer to force extra time, but Mario Kempes and a deafening home crowd proved too much, and Netherlands fell 3–1 — back-to-back runners-up, still without a trophy.
1978.homeColors: orange shirt, white shorts, orange socks (Adidas)

1990.headline: Three Draws, Then Out
1990.story: A flat title defense: three straight 1–1 or scoreless draws left the reigning European champions stumbling into the Round of 16 as one of the better third-placed teams. Ronald Koeman's free kick wasn't enough — West Germany, the eventual champions, eliminated the Dutch 2–1 in Milan, a game remembered for Frank Rijkaard's infamous spit at Rudi Völler.
1990.homeColors: orange shirt, white shorts, orange socks (Adidas)

1994.headline: Bergkamp's Breakout Summer
1994.story: Dennis Bergkamp scored in three straight matches, including the winner over the Republic of Ireland in the Round of 16, announcing himself as a genuine superstar. Brazil, on their way to a fourth title, edged the Dutch 3–2 in a wide-open Dallas quarter-final that came down to who finished their chances better.
1994.homeColors: orange shirt, white shorts, orange socks (Lotto)

1998.headline: Bergkamp's Touch, Then Heartbreak
1998.story: Edgar Davids' 90th-minute winner over FR Yugoslavia and Dennis Bergkamp's sublime touch-and-finish against Argentina sent the Netherlands to the semi-final. Brazil survived on penalties after a 1–1 draw, and a tired Dutch side lost the third-place match to Croatia, closing a heartbreaking summer just short of the podium.
1998.homeColors: orange shirt, black shorts, orange socks (Lotto)

2006.headline: The Battle of Nuremberg
2006.story: A tense, error-strewn 0–0 draw with Argentina set the tone for a knockout meeting with Portugal that turned violent — four red cards and 16 yellows in a game remembered as the Battle of Nuremberg. Maniche's strike was the only goal, and Netherlands went home having played some of the most heavily-officiated football in World Cup history.
2006.homeColors: orange shirt, white shorts, orange socks (Nike)

2010.headline: Iniesta Breaks Dutch Hearts
2010.story: Wesley Sneijder scored five goals to drag the Dutch to their third final, beating Brazil and Uruguay along the way without ever losing control of a match. In Johannesburg, a bruising, foul-riddled final against Spain went scoreless into extra time before Andrés Iniesta struck in the 116th minute, denying Netherlands the trophy yet again.
2010.homeColors: orange shirt, white shorts, orange socks (Nike)
2010.awayColors: blue shirt, blue shorts, blue socks (Nike)

2014.headline: Revenge Rout, Bronze Reward
2014.story: Louis van Gaal's side opened by dismantling defending champions Spain 5–1 — Robin van Persie's diving header the highlight — and rolled unbeaten into the semi-final before penalty shootouts eliminated them against Costa Rica and then Argentina. A 3–0 win over Brazil in the third-place match delivered Netherlands' first World Cup bronze.
2014.homeColors: orange shirt, white shorts, orange socks (Nike)
2014.awayColors: blue shirt with orange trim, blue shorts (Nike)

2022.headline: Weghorst's Double, Messi's Answer
2022.story: Cody Gakpo scored in three straight group games to introduce himself to the world, and Denzel Dumfries capped a 3–1 Round of 16 win over the United States. Wout Weghorst's stoppage-time double forced extra time against Argentina in the quarter-final, but Messi's side prevailed on penalties, ending Van Gaal's second spell in charge.
2022.homeColors: orange shirt, black shorts, black socks (Nike)
